content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • • Administration • • • Executive/Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5862         322-5869         322-5931         322-6441         322-6444

June 28, 1979

Mr. Nancy L. Williams
Member, Governing Board
San Jose Community College
District
4750 San Felipe Road
San Jose, CA 95121

Dear Mr. Williams:

This letter is in response to your letter of May 29, 1979,
and Mr. Goff's letter of April 30, 1979, concerning your obligations under the Political Reform Act.  The facts as you related
them are as follows.

You are a member of the Governing Board of the San Jose
Community College District. You also are a salesman for Zahorik
Brokerage Firm which brokers tax sheltered annuities on behalf
of several insurance companies. Some of the District's employees
have purchased policies sufficiently large to provide you with
commissions of more than $250 per year.

The District is indirectly involved in the purchase of
these tax sheltered annuities in two ways.  First, these particular annuities can only be purchased by public employees.
Therefore, if a person's employment status with the District
is terminated, his benefits are frozen as of that point and
he may not continue to make monthly payments to purchase annuities.  Second, the District has adopted a policy permitting
employees to authorize payroll deductions for the purchase of
such annuities.  Any qualifying insurance company may offer
annuities to the District's employees through the payroll
deduction plan so long as the annuity contract makes it clear
that the District has no liability with respect to any default
or breach by a District employee.

Mr. Yancy L. Williams
June 28, 1979                                                      Page Two


     From time to time, the Governing Board makes decisions
involving general salary increases for all employees, merit
salary increases for specific employees and personnel decisions
which sometimes result in the termination of a person's employ-
ent with the District. You have asked whether you have any
obligations under the Political Reform Act to report your income
from the sale of annuities on your statements of economic inter-
ests or any obligation to disqualify yourself from participating
in the types of decisions described above.

     Pursuant to the Conflict of Interest Code for the San Jose
Community College District, governing board members are required
to disclose sources of income which "...are contractors or
subcontractors which are or have been within the previous two
year period engaged in the performance of building construction
or design within the District" and "...income from persons or
business entities engaged in the acquisition or disposal of
real property within the jurisdiction."1/ Unless the employees
who have purchased the annuities from you fall into one of
these disclosure categories, you need not report them as sources
of income on your Statement of Economic Interests.

     In addition, the District's Conflict of Interest Code and
Government Code section 87100 provides that no public official
shall make, participate in the making or use his official posi-
tion to influence the making of a governmental decision in which
he has a financial interest.2/ The term "financial interest"
is defined in Section 87103 as follows:

     An official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from
its effect on the public generally, on:

          ...(c) Any source of income, other than loans by
     a commercial lending institution in the regular
     course of business, aggregating two hundred fifty
     dollars ($250) or more in value received by or
     promised to the public official within twelve months
     prior to the time when the decision is made;...


1/ The Code also requires the disclosure of investments and
interests in real property but such disclosure is not relevant
to the questions posed in this letter

2/ All statutory references are to the Government Code
unless otherwise noted.

Mr. Yancy L. Williams
June 28, 1979                                                Page Three


     In the instant case, employees who purchase a sufficient
amount of annuities to provide you with $250 or more in commis-
sions during a year are sources of income to you. Accordingly,
you should disqualify yourself from making, participating in the
making or using your official position to influence any govern-
mental decision which foreseeably will have a material financial
effect on such persons distinguishable from its effects on the
public generally. Specifically, you should not participate in
decisions concerning whether a person who is a source of income
to you should be granted a merit increase.3/ In addition, you
should not participate in any other personnel decision which
will significantly affect a person who is a source of income to
you. For example, you should not participate in a decision to
reclassify the position of or lay off such a person.

     Lastly, we come to the question of whether you may make or
participate in decisions regarding across the board cost of
living increases for the District's staff. At present, we do
not have enough information to answer this question but we have
undertaken an investigation to gather the necessary facts. In
the interim, I would advise you, out of an abundance of caution,
to disqualify yourself from voting on or participating in decisions
concerning across the board cost of living increases for District
staff members. Once we have gathered the necessary factual mater-
ial, I will write you another letter providing you with permanent
advice on this issue.

     If you have further questions, please contact me.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [illegible]
                                        Ted Prim, Chief
                                        Conflicts of Interest Division


_____________________________
3/ It is my understanding that the staff prepares a list of
persons which it recommends for merit increases. You may wish to
have the list separated into two lists -- one containing persons
who are not sources of income to you and the other containing
persons who are sources of income to you. In that way, it would
be easy for you to participate in the discussion concerning merit
increases for those persons who are not sources of income to you
and to disqualify yourself from considering merit increases to
those persons who are sources of income to you.

79128                                                    4750 San Felipe Road
                                                                  San Jose, California 95131
                                                                  (408) 274-6700

SAN JOSE COMMUNITY COLLEGE DISTRICT
                                                                  Otto Roemmieh, Chancellor

                                                                  San Jose City College
                                                                  Evergreen Valley College


                                          April 30, 1979


Mr. Thomas Houston, Chairman
Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Dear Mr. Houston:

     A member of the San Jose Community College District Board of
Trustees engages in the business of selling tax sheltered annuities.
Several of his clients are employees of San Jose Community College
District.

     A member of our faculty has charged that because several of our
employees choose to buy tax sheltered annuities through this Trustee,
there is a conflict of interest when that Trustee discusses salary issues
or votes upon salaries to be given to staff. The concept being that a
salary increase granted to, say the Superintendent, may later be used
to purchase a tax sheltered annuity from the Trustee, and a sales com-
mission is earned by the Trustee. This is the alleged conflict of in-
terest, and it is believed by the objecting faculty member that the
Trustee should excuse himself from all salary discussions in executive
sessions and refrain from voting on salary issues.

     The Board of Trustees has only one adopted policy related to tax
sheltered annuities: "An employee may authorize deductions from his
warrant for United Fund, employee association or union dues, tax
sheltered annuities, dependent coverage on District insurance plans
and credit unions."

     In another section of our policy book the following has been adopted:
"It shall be the responsibility of the instructor to inform and authorize
the Business Office to make individual salary deductions he/she may
elect for professional organization dues, life or other insurance, tax
sheltered annuity, credit union, or others that have been authorized by
the Board of Trustees." That is total involvement of the Board policy
in the tax sheltered annuities area.


Board of Trustees - Charles R. Blackmore • Robert M. Giuli • John E. Marlow, M.D. • Esther S. Ono • Yancy L. Williams

Thomas Houston                     -2-                    April 30, 1979


    The Board of Trustees and the Business Services Division of the
District has chosen to take an "arm's length" approach toward whom
an employee selects to provide a tax sheltered annuity. An employee
may select any salesman, broker or company which will sign our hold
harmless clause and there is no authorized officer or employee of the
District who endorses or advocates the selection of any particular firm,
agent or broker. Free selection by the employee is our approach. How-
ever, the District does enter into contract with the firms selected by the
employee, and if the firms selected are represented by our Board member,
and if he is the agent who made the sale to our staff, he then receives a
paid commission.

    The questions we have are:

    1.  Does a conflict of interest exist with the member of our
        Board of Trustees related to his actions selling tax
        sheltered annuities to employees of the District?

    2.  If "yes," can he sit in on discussions of salary and vote
        on salary matters?

    May we please have your formal response in writing as soon as
possible. If you need additional information, please feel free to call.

                                                            Sincerely,


                                                R. W. Goff, Vice Chancellor
                                                Administrative Services

RWG:cf

cc: Otto Roemmich
    Yancy Williams
    John Marlow

4750 San Felipe Road
San Jose, California 95121
(408) 274-6709

SAN JOSE COMMUNITY COLLEGE DISTRICT

Otto Acemmich, Chancellor

San Jose City College
Evergreen Valley College

May 29, 1979

Mr. Ted Prim
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Mr. Prim:

This letter will confirm our conversation earlier this morning relating to the
April 30 letter from the San Jose Community College District which was signed
by R. W. Goff, Vice Chancellor, Administrative Services.

I am the Trustee against whom the faculty member has had the conflict of
interest charge and am requesting that the letter referred to above be
considered as my personal request.

The merit pay increases granted to a part of the administrative staff included
some of my clients. Other administrators, who are also my clients, did not
receive the merit pay increases. Also, the merit pay or salary increases given
to all the District employees did not and have not increased my commission in
any amount.

The California Education Code grants employees of the public school system the
right of insurance company selection to carry their annuity investments and the
Internal Revenue Service's regulations qualify them as being eligible and also
establish the formula as to the amount they may invest.

Thank you for your interest.

Sincerely,

[signature]
Yancy L. Williams
Member, Governing Board

YLW:mp

Board of Trustees
Curtis R Blackmore • James A Cosley • Robert M Goff • John P Mahan, M.D. • Fisher S. Ono • Yancy L. Williams

Mr. Phil Crawford
13602 Saratoga-Los Gatos Road
Los Gatos, California 95030

June 13, 1979


Mr. Ted Prim
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 "K" Street Building
Sacramento, California 95804

Dear Ted:

Pursuant to our discussion I will attempt to outline the issues which
the various segmental interests in our district feel constitute a violation
of the Political Reform Act by Trustee Yancy Williams.

First of all a failure on the part of Mr. Williams to disclose his
brokerage business endeavors involving numerous tax sheltered annuity
corporations and the community college employers on his required conflict
of interest statement.

Secondly, Mr. Williams has solicited by telephone District Administrative
staff members to join his brokers and has also utilized the privileged
phone directories and mailing lists of the District to obtain personnel data
for his own business endeavors. Subsequent to which he has contacted various
employees individually in attempts to sell annuities to these individuals
under implicit rewards of career development and job security in return for
his own financial gain. Mr. Williams has further distributed pamphlets and
personal business literature utilizing the privileged District staff
correspondence and facilities. Mr. Williams has also utilized District
travel functions paid for out of public tax funds to promote, negotiate,
and discuss his own personal business endeavors.

Further, Mr. Williams has participated in individual personnel lay off
deliberations and official actions where decisions were made pitting the
collective merits of lay off employees with them having lost a potential
interest against the merits of having off those whom he did not have a
financial interest in dismissing. As a result we have found no case where
an employee holding a financial interest with Williams Company has ever
been laid off or dismissed. We are aware of past recommendations to lay
off employees whom Mr. Williams did have a fiduciary relationship with and
all of these recommendations were rejected by board deliberations heavily
influenced by Mr. Williams participation and official actions.

TO: Ted Prim
Fair Political Practices Commission
June 13, 1979
Page 2 of 2

Recently Mr. Williams participated in the individual-by-individual
negotiations for salary increases involving administrative staff members
(merit pay raises) and the result included raises for three of the top
five Contract Administrators who have purchased annuities with Mr. Williams.
The persons specifically include the chancellor, the Vice Chancellor of
Educational Services, and the President of Evergreen Valley College.

Mr. Williams has also secured the assistance of one of the District
Administrators who provides a front role by soliciting colleagues to
secure annuities through Mr. Williams firm. This employee was recommended
for a lay off but this recommendation was rejected at the Board level in
spite of the staff analysis.

Finally, we feel Mr. Williams' financial gains from the District
employees salary annuities are broadly distributed in the employee population and that a disclosure of his exact profits from such endeavors should
be required. Further, we feel that Mr. Williams should be barred from any
future personnel actions of any kind which affect changes in salary for
employees because of said changes in any variance serve to either enhance
the need of Mr. Williams personal business or retard such need. We also
feel that Mr. Williams should not participate in any salary negotiations
involving employees in any class or unit wherein he has investment in the
portion of these salaries which is designated for investment in the corporations which he brokers. Any increase in employee salaries granted by
public funds can serve to increase the specific amounts of revenue he receive
and also the potential field of clients he represents. It is our opinion
that an elected official should serve the interest of the community and not
those interest which provide him a financial profit by distribution of public
funds.

We further request full investigation, prosecution, and civil action for
these and all other violations which our limited investigation has failed
to uncover. The constituents represented by the undersigned includes
representatives of the San Jose City College, Faculty Senate, the Evergreen
College Faculty Council, the California Teachers Association, the Santa Clara
Valley Black Caucus, the West Valley Taxpayers Coalition, and the San Jose
Community College Taxpayers Association.

Sincerely,

Phil J. Crawford

PJC:er

sections.question: You have asked whether you have any obligations under the Political Reform Act to report your income from the sale of annuities on your statements of economic interests or any obligation to disqualify yourself from participating in the types of decisions described above.
sections.conclusion: In the instant case, employees who purchase a sufficient amount of annuities to provide you with $250 or more in commissions during a year are sources of income to you. Accordingly, you should disqualify yourself from making, participating in the making or using your official position to influence any governmental decision which foreseeably will have a material financial effect on such persons distinguishable from its effects on the public generally. Specifically, you should not participate in decisions concerning whether a person who is a source of income to you should be granted a merit increase. In addition, you should not participate in any other personnel decision which will significantly affect a person who is a source of income to you.
sections.question_synthetic: Does a governing board member who sells tax-sheltered annuities to district employees have obligations to disclose this income and recuse himself from salary and personnel decisions affecting those employees under the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The board member must disqualify himself from decisions affecting employees who are sources of $250+ in annual commissions, including merit increases and significant personnel decisions. Regarding across-the-board cost of living increases, an investigation was needed before providing final guidance, and he was advised to disqualify himself pending further advice.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains multiple related correspondence (incoming requests from Goff and Williams, plus external complaint from Crawford) leading to the formal FPPC advice letter from Ted Prim dated June 28, 1979. Question and conclusion sections synthesized from body text as no formal labeled sections exist. Final paragraph indicates investigation ongoing regarding across-the-board raises.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You have asked whether you have any obligations under the Political Reform Act to report your income from the sale of annuities on your statements of economic interests or any obligation to disqualify yourself from participating in the types of decisions described above.

CONCLUSION: In the instant case, employees who purchase a sufficient amount of annuities to provide you with $250 or more in commissions during a year are sources of income to you. Accordingly, you should disqualify yourself from making, participating in the making or using your official position to influence any governmental decision which foreseeably will have a material financial effect on such persons distinguishable from its effects on the public generally. Specifically, you should not participate in decisions concerning whether a person who is a source of income to you should be granted a merit increase. In addition, you should not participate in any other personnel decision which will significantly affect a person who is a source of income to you.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises a San Jose Community College District governing board member who sells tax-sheltered annuities to district employees that he must disqualify himself from personnel and salary decisions affecting customers who provide him $250+ in annual commissions, but need not report them unless they fall into specific disclosure categories under the district's conflict of interest code.
